Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Granting Preference In Setting Case For Trial, Advancing The Current Trial Date; And Extending Discovery Cutoff
Off Calendar Feb-25-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances, not contested, and the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Plaintiff's Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is GRANTED under C.C.P. 36(b). Opposition filed by WW Grainger, Inc; Dayton Electric Manufacturing; and Cal Steam, Inc. Joinder filed by Ford Motor Company and Williams Furnace Company. Reply filed.

C.C.P 36(b) provides, in pertinent part, that a civil action to recover damages for wrongful death or personal injury shall be entitled to preference upon the motion of any party to the action who is under the age of fourteen years and has a substantial interest in the case. Here, Decedent's son, Dennison Udovch, is entitled to preference because he is under the age of fourteen and a plaintiff in his father's wrongful death case. Defendants argue that granting this motion would hamper their ability to complete discovery, bring dispositive motions, and adequately prepare for trial. However, the provisions of C.C.P. 36(b) are mandatory and absolute, as courts have found under subdivision (a) of the same statute. "Where a party meets the requisite standard for calendar preference under [Code of Civil Procedure section 36] subdivision (a), preference must be granted. No weighing of interests is involved." (Fox v. Superior Court (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 529, 535; see also, e.g., Miller v. Superior Court (1990) 221 Cal.App.3d 1200, 1204 [statute "grants a mandatory and absolute right to trial preference"] Swaithes v. Superior Court (1989) 212 Cal.App.3d 1082, 1085 [trial court "has no power to balance the different interests of opposing litigants in applying the provision."].)

1. The trial date is June 9, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206.
a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is June 25, 2025.
b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20.

2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is June 3, 2025.
a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise.
b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation.
c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing.

3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days.
a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier.
b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter.

4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service.
5. The fact discovery cut-off date is May 23, 2025.
6. The expert discovery cut-off date is June 6, 2025.

7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than March 27, 2025.
a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission.
b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. (Part 1 of 2) (Mini minutes continue, see next entry)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE - TRIAL COURTS
Off Calendar Jun-11-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Andrew Chew, Esq. of Brayton Purcell, Tel. 415.898.1555, appearing for the plaintiff;
Laura Przetak, Esq. of Spanos Przetak, Tel. 510.250.0200, appearing as Designated Defense Counsel.

Hearing re: Case Management Conference set pursuant to the preference order filed on April 2, 2024.

The Court and counsel confer regarding the notice of death, notice of stay, and the change in procedures when a plaintiff has passed away.

The Court states that going forward in these instances where a plaintiff has passed away that an ex parte appliccation will be required, that counsel is to proceed by way of an ex parte application that may be made either by way of an appearance on a Tuesday at 11:00 a.m. on the Court's ex parte calendar or by way of ex parte application drop off. The ex parte application is to be supported by a declaration with a death certificate, the request for stay should be no longer than six months, the application should set forth all dates that counsel is requesting to vacate, and the application should set forth a date on the Court's trial setting conference calendar not to exceed six months.

Additional discsussions are held. Mr. Chew expressed a few concerns with the new procedure such as locating heirs and noted that it is hard to pivot on a dime. Ms. Przetak stated that she is part of a Bay Area Asbestos monthly committee and noted that she can alert the Bay Area Bar at that monthly meeting.

At the conclusion of discussions, Ms. Przetak is to prepare an ex parte application to vacate the mandatory settlement conference date of July 18, 2024. The stay in this case is through December 19, 2024, which is the current date set on the Court's Asbestos Trial Setting Conference calendar. Zoom appearances on December 19, 2024, are acceptable.

The Court noted that the change in procedure will help to maintain a clear record in the Court's docket.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff: Not Present. (301/RCE)
